summary: Italian Renaissance painter, court artist to the Gonzaga family in Mantua. Known for the Dead Christ (extreme foreshortening), Camera degli Sposi (di sotto in su ceiling), Triumphs of Caesar, St. Sebastian (three versions), and San Zeno Altarpiece.

works.name: Lamentation over the Dead Christ
works.description: Extremely foreshortened body of Christ on a marble slab with prominent nail wounds on feet. Mary, St. John, and Mary Magdalene weep at the left. In Milan's Brera Gallery. Exemplifies foreshortening technique with large feet and small head to illustrate Christ lying away from the viewer. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Extremely foreshortened body of Christ on marble slab; prominent nail wounds on hands and feet; two/three mourners at left (Mary, John, Magdalene)
examples: the central figure's hands and feet are propped up to show his wounds while resting on a marble slab

works.name: Camera degli Sposi (c. 1465-1474)
works.description: Frescoed room in the Ducal Palace of Mantua for patron Ludovico Gonzaga. Ceiling uses di sotto in su technique to depict an oculus opening to a blue sky with putti. Court Scene depicts Gonzaga family. Among the first Renaissance illusionistic ceilings. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Di sotto in su ceiling with oculus opening to blue sky, surrounded by putti; peacock, turbaned man looking down
examples: uses the di sotto in su technique to depict an oculus opening up to a cloudy sky
A peacock and a man in a turban are depicted next to several foreshortened cherubs
putti stand around a circular opening; that painting was executed by Andrea Mantegna for the Camera degli Sposi

clues.clue: Portraits of first eight Roman emperors as grisaille roundels; artist's self-portrait among floral vines
examples: marble putti holding up wreathed portraits of the first eight Roman emperors

clues.clue: In the Palazzo Ducale in Mantua; originally called Camera Picta (not actually a bridal chamber)

clues.clue: Among the first Renaissance illusionistic ceilings; di sotto in su means 'seen from below' / 'from below, upward'
examples: This artwork painted by Andrea Mantegna is among the first Renaissance illusionistic ceilings

works.name: Triumphs of Caesar (c. 1484-1492)
works.description: Nine-panel series depicting Caesar's victory procession. Now at Hampton Court Palace. Includes SPQR banners, slaves, vases, spoils. Caesar rides in an ornate chariot. Wikipedia

works.name: St. Sebastian (three versions)
works.description: Three paintings of St. Sebastian tied to a column/arch, riddled with arrows. Located in Vienna, Venice, and the Louvre. One version has a rider/death figure in the clouds. Painted to ward off the plague in Mantua. Replaced central tree with a marble column and depicted Celestial Jerusalem in the background. Wikipedia

works.name: San Zeno Altarpiece (1456-1459)
works.description: Triptych for the Basilica of San Zeno in Verona. Four Corinthian columns divide three scenes connected by hanging garlands. Central predella depicts the Crucifixion. Pseudo-Kufic script on Mary's halo. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Four Corinthian columns divide three scenes connected by hanging garlands; sacra conversazione; for Basilica of San Zeno in Verona

clues.clue: Predellas stolen by the French and replaced with copies

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Court artist to the Gonzaga family in Mantua. Born near Padua. Brother-in-law of Giovanni Bellini. Other works: Parnassus (Mars and Venus on arch; for Isabella d'Este's studiolo), Triumph of the Virtues / Minerva Driving Away Vice, Judith and Holofernes (androgynous figure in blue toga), St. James Led to His Execution (destroyed 1944, Ovetari Chapel), Agony in the Garden (Judas leads procession along winding path), Descent into Limbo (attributed 2018), Adoration of the Magi, Battle of the Sea Gods. Lorenzo Costa finished his Reign of Comus after his death.

comprehensive_summary:
Andrea Mantegna (1431-1506) was an Italian Renaissance painter born near Padua who became the court artist to the Gonzaga family in Mantua. He was the brother-in-law of Giovanni Bellini. He is most commonly identified by The Dead Christ (extreme foreshortening) and the Camera degli Sposi.

The Lamentation over the Dead Christ is his most frequently clued work. It shows an extremely foreshortened body on a marble slab with prominent nail wounds on the hands and feet, with large feet and a small head illustrating the figure lying away from the viewer. Mary, St. John, and Mary Magdalene weep at the left. It is housed in Milan's Brera Gallery. Man Lying on a Slab is a preparatory study drawing.

The Camera degli Sposi (c. 1465-1474) is a frescoed room in the Ducal Palace of Mantua, among the first Renaissance illusionistic ceilings. Its ceiling uses the di sotto in su technique (meaning 'from below, upward') depicting an oculus opening to a blue sky surrounded by putti, a peacock, and a turbaned man. The Court Scene shows patron Ludovico Gonzaga turning in his chair to speak to his secretary, with his family including a dwarf. The ceiling includes grisaille roundels of the first eight Roman emperors and the artist's self-portrait. Originally called Camera Picta, it is not actually a bridal chamber despite its name.

The Triumphs of Caesar (c. 1484-1492) is a nine-panel series depicting Caesar's victory procession with SPQR banners, slaves, vases, and spoils, now at Hampton Court Palace. Caesar rides in an ornate chariot celebrating his military victories in Gaul. A man carries a cage of exotic birds while soldiers lean on a marble statue. A preparatory sketch discovered in 2020 shows two bugle players with SPQR banners, and Andrea Andreani produced a 12-foot-wide chiaroscuro print of the series.

His three versions of St. Sebastian (Vienna, Venice, Louvre) show the saint tied to a column pierced by arrows. One version has a scythe-carrying rider (Death) in the clouds with anachronistic Roman ruins behind. The paintings were made to ward off the plague in Mantua. Mantegna replaced the traditional central tree with a marble column and depicted the Celestial Jerusalem on a mountain in the background.

The San Zeno Altarpiece (1456-1459) is a triptych for the Basilica of San Zeno in Verona with four Corinthian columns dividing three scenes connected by hanging garlands, the Crucifixion as the central predella, and pseudo-Kufic script on Mary's halo. Its predellas were stolen by the French and replaced with copies.

Other important works include Parnassus (Mars and Venus on a natural arch, for Isabella d'Este's studiolo, with Anteros shooting a blow dart at Vulcan's genitals), Triumph of the Virtues / Minerva Driving Away Vice, St. James Led to His Execution (destroyed in 1944 bombing of Ovetari Chapel in Padua, featuring an extremely low worm's-eye viewpoint where St. James blesses a man), Agony in the Garden (three sleeping men, vulture on bare tree, Judas leading a procession), Judith and Holofernes (Judith depicted androgynously in blue toga), Descent into Limbo (attributed to Mantegna in 2018), Adoration of the Magi (Balthazar in leopard-skin turban), and engravings like Battle of the Sea Gods. Lorenzo Costa finished The Reign of Comus after Mantegna's death for Isabella d'Este's studiolo.
